assignment_description:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: US Atlas, Globe, or Wall Map

PREP: Print Blank World Map and Blank North America Map
∞ Map Link: Blank World Map
∞ Map Link: Blank North America Map

➜ INTRO
In geography this year, we will journey across the USA. Geography is learning about different places around the world. All kinds of maps are used to help us in the study of geography—globes, wall maps, and atlases.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Find the United States of America.
2\. Find the equator. Is the United States north or south of the equator?
3\. What is to the west of the United States? (Pacific Ocean) To the east? (Atlantic Ocean) To the north? (Canada) To the south? (Mexico and the Gulf of Mexico)

➜ MAP WORK
Add the Blank World Map (referred to moving forward as "My World Map") and the Blank North America Map (referred to moving forward as "My North America Map") to your geography journal.
⍞ Map Resource: My World Map
1\. Add the title "My World Map."
2\. Color in the United States of America.
⍞ Map Resource: My North America Map
1\. Add title "My North America Map."
2\. Label the borders of the contiguous United States. (Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean, Gulf of Mexico, Canada, Mexico)

➜ READ & NARRATE
United States Atlas
p.14 "Like a giant" - "single circle."

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: US Atlas "The Political United States", p.14-15
1\. Locate California, and specifically Los Angeles, California, on the West Coast.
2\. Locate Maryland and Washington, D.C. on the East Coast.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My North America Map
1\. Add Los Angeles (with a dot) and Washington, D.C. (with a star inside a circle) to the map and label each.
2\. Label the United States in the Northern Area of the USA.

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Depending on where you live, it may rain often or not much at all. Please start to take notes on where the water goes when it rains. Look for where it goes near your home, and even see if you can follow it in your neighborhood. The notes can go in your nature or geography journal.

Meet the Pages
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, Blank U.S. Map, Geography journal

PREP: Print Blank U.S. Map (referred to moving forward as "My U.S. Map")
∞ Map Link: Blank U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
Let's look at the map in the front of the Cross-Country book. What do you notice?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Inside Cover Cross-Country
Locate the red line. This book is about a family that travels along that line (route) across the United States.
1\. Locate your state. Does the trip go through your state? If not, how many states away from the red line do you live?
2\. Locate Los Angeles and place your finger on it. Trace your finger from there all the way across the United States to Washington, D.C.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.6-7 "The man" - "it will rain!"

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Add the Blank US Map to your geography journal.
2\. Add the title "My US Map" (making sure the title does not cover any states).
3\. Add Los Angeles and Washington, D.C.
4\. Label California.

Compass
⍞ Materials: compass, paper, tape

➜ DISCUSS
There are naturally occurring magnetic rocks called magnetite, but modern compasses use a magnetized needle. Both magnetite and a modern magnetic needle, when allowed to move freely, will swing to point north. Today, we will use a compass to find direction in your classroom or room in your home.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Place the compass in the middle of the room and allow it to stop moving.
2\. The needle will be pointing north. You must move the N sign on the compass to line up with the point of the needle.
3\. On a piece of paper, write N for north and tape it to the wall that the compass is pointing to.
4\. When you face north, east will be on your right, west on your left, and south directly behind you.
5\. Using these directions, make more sheets with E, W, and S on them and tape them to your walls.

➜ PLAY
Using the directions on the walls, take turns with another student or teacher, following directions such as "take 3 steps east" or "take 2 giant steps south." You can even make it into a Mother May I game.

Compass Rose
⍞ Materials: compass, chalk

➜ DISCUSS
During the last lesson, we found direction inside, and today we will find direction outside and create a compass rose.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Draw a large circle on a paved area. Place the compass in the middle and allow it to stop moving.
2\. The needle will be pointing north. You must move the N sign on the compass to line up with the point of the needle.
3\. Draw a line from the N side of the compass straight to the edge of the circle, and mark it with an N.
4\. Do the same thing with E, W, and S.
5\. Today, we will add more directions to the compass rose. Halfway between the N and the E, you will write a NE, which stands for Northeast, on the circle. Halfway between N and W, you will write NW, which stands for Northwest. Continue by adding a SE between S and E, for southeast, and add SW between S and W, for southwest.
6\. You will notice that it is easier to give more accurate directions because you have more to refer to.

➜ PLAY
1\. Using the directions on the compass rose, take turns with another student or teacher, following directions such as "take 3 steps northeast" or "take 2 giant steps southwest." This can be played like Mother May I.
2\. Another game you can play is to give directions to an object, like a tree, outside by saying things like "take 5 steps west, and then 2 steps northwest. What is there?"

Compass Rose and Map Key
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-7 "You've moved" - "these tools!"

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: Making Your Own Maps, various pages
1\. Can you find the compass rose on p.7? Which directions are on it?
2\. Locate the keys on p.5 and 7. What symbols do they use? How are they the same or different?
3\. Look at keys on maps on p.15,16,19, and 21. How are they the same or different?

Airport
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.8-9 "One night" - "ask for it!"

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas, "The Political United States: Population" p.16-17
1\. Locate the map keys (there are 2), as well as Los Angeles, CA, and Washington, D.C.
2\. What do the colors mean on the rightmost map key? How do the color symbols change with the numbers? (Larger numbers are represented by darker colors.)
3\. What do the symbols mean in the other map key? How do they change with the number ranges? (The symbols get larger as the population increases.)
4\. What do you notice about the areas near Los Angeles and Washington, D.C.?
5\. Find your home state and general location. How does your area's population compare with Los Angeles or Washington, D.C.?

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My U.S. Map
1\. Add your city (or closest town) to your map, and label it.

Over Los Angeles
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country

➜ INTRO
Have you ever flown on a plane? Could you see out the window? What did it look like?

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: Illustrations on p.10, 11, and 12 of Cross-Country
1\. Look at p.10 and p.11. What do you notice on each one? (Do you see streets, cars, houses, and large buildings?)
2\. Which picture is taken further up in the air? (p.11) How can you tell? (buildings look smaller)
3\. What are the "wrinkly" bits? (mountains) Can you see any other natural things? (possibly a river, fields)
4\. I wonder why the city stops where it does? (possibly the mountains)
5\. Turn to p.12. What is different about this illustration? (It is mainly water.) What can you see? (boats, ports, roads, buildings)
6\. Is the illustration on p.12 closer or farther away than the previous illustrations? How can you tell?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.10-12 "It was a little" - "see the people."

Types of Maps Intro

➜ INTRO
We are going to spend a few lessons learning about different types of maps.

➜ DISCUSS
1\. Today, we will look at the first 2 types of maps. The first we will call a model or 3-dimensional map. Last year, you used blocks (or some other objects) to make maps. You did this with your room, your home, your neighbors, etc.
2\. The second type of map we will call a pictorial view. It is a drawing of a street-level view of a home, building, etc. You also drew these last year when you drew your home and neighboring homes/buildings.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the linked map pictures.
1\. Look at the linked Room Model and Model Showing Orientation.
∞ Image link: Room Model
∞ Image link: Model Showing Orientation
2\. Make a quick model of a room in your home or school.
3\. Look at the linked pictorial view of a house. It will be at eye level, and drawn as if you are looking directly at it.
∞ Image link: Pictorial map
4\. If time permits, draw a pictorial view of your home from the outside.

Types of Maps Continued
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ RECAP
What types of maps did we discuss in the last lesson?

➜ DISCUSS
1\. Today, we will look at the next 2 types of maps. The first we will call a panoramic 3D map. Imagine you are a bird high up in a tree looking down at your home; what kind of drawing would that make? How would it look different from the street view of your home?
2\. The second type of map we will call an aerial view. This time, imagine you are a bird flying directly over your home; what kind of drawing would that make? (could only see the roof) How is this different than the panoramic view?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the linked map pictures as well as one in Making Your Own Maps.
1\. Look at the linked Panoramic 3D map, and have the student identify its type.
∞ Map Link: Panoramic 3D map
2\. Look at the linked Aerial View map, and have the student identify its type.
∞ Map Link: Aerial View map
3\. Look at Making Your Own Maps p.16. What type of map is this, based on what we studied today? (panoramic)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Don't forget to keep an eye out when it rains. Try to pay attention to a light rain versus a hard rain. Don't forget to keep notes in your geography or nature journal.

Types of Maps, Continued
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ RECAP
What types of maps did we discuss in the last lesson?

➜ DISCUSS
Today, we will look at the last type of map we will be discussing this year. This type of map is a symbolic map. These can vary greatly. Making Your Own Maps is full of symbolic maps. As you read today, look for maps that are not models, pictorial, panoramic, or aerial.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-15 "Say you" - "stands for."

➜ DISCUSS
Did you find symbol maps? (p.5, 7, 8, 11, 13, 15) Note: If there is confusion about why some of those are not pictorial or panoramic, look back at the previous 2 lessons.

The Pages Are Moving
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
Do you know what kinds of plants grow near your home?

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas, "The Political United States: Political Map" p.14-15
1\. Locate Los Angeles, CA, and Denver, CO.
2\. Remember the approximate locations of these cities, as you will mark them lightly on the next map.
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas "The Physical United States: Physical Map" p.8-9
1\. Mark lightly the approximate locations of Los Angeles and Denver.
2\. Find the map key; what do the colors mean? (vegetation/biome)
3\. What do you notice about the areas around Los Angeles and Denver? Are they the same or different?
4\. Find your home state and general location. What is the vegetation near you? Compare it to those near Los Angeles and Denver.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.13 "It was big" - "buy this place."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My US Map
1\. Add Denver, CO
2\. Add anything else of interest. (Could add a small illustration of what grows in each place.)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Go for a walk in your neighborhood. What grows near you? What type of vegetation (trees, bushes, flowers, etc.) grows near you?

Compass Rose and Directions
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, My U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
We have talked about directions in your room/classroom and outdoors. Today, we will learn about directions on a map.

➜ MAP QUESTION
∞ Map Link: Map of the US with a compass rose
1\. Find the compass rose on the linked map of the US.
2\. What direction is north? south? east? west?
3\. Based on those directions, add a compass rose to the map in the front of Cross-Country. (You can draw directly on the page or use a small sticky note.)
4\. Using the map in Cross-Country, what is to the west of Los Angeles? (Pacific Ocean)
5\. What ocean is east of Washington, D.C.? (Atlantic Ocean)
6\. If we travel from Los Angeles to Washington, D.C., which direction would you travel? (east)
7\. Where do you live? Which direction would you go to get to Washington, D.C.?

➜ MAP WORK
Add a compass rose to your map.

Islands
⍞ Materials: World Wall Map or Globe

➜ MAP QUESTION
1\. Locate your home on the globe.
2\. Locate the Equator. Do you live north or south of it?
3\. Which continent do you live on?
4\. Which ocean is closest to you?
5\. What is an island? (A body of land surrounded by water.)
6\. Locate a very large island in the Atlantic Ocean. (The largest island is Greenland, which extends from the Arctic Ocean into the North Atlantic Ocean. Other islands are listed in the next question.)
7\. Can you locate any more islands in the Atlantic Ocean? (Possibilities include Iceland, Ireland, Great Britain, Newfoundland, and the Falklands, among others.)
8\. Some islands in the Pacific Ocean are isolated, while others exist in groups. Can you locate a group of islands in the Pacific Ocean? (Aleutian Islands, Hawaii, Galápagos Islands, Japan, Philippine Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, Marshall Islands, Micronesia, Solomon Islands, Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and New Zealand, among many others.)
9\. Can you locate standalone islands in the Pacific that are not part of a group? (This question is more of a scavenger hunt, and the student may only name Vancouver Island and Taiwan. Searching for individual islands in the Pacific Ocean makes it apparent that almost all islands in this ocean are part of a larger group.)
10\. What is an island close to you?
11\. Do you see any islands close to Los Angeles?

Catalina Island
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas

➜ INTRO
Charlotte Mason defined an island as follows in her book, Elementary Geography. "The high parts of the ocean bed rise above water in many places in the Pacific. These are called islands. An island is just a piece of land with water all around it. On the map, you will find many islands scattered over the Pacific Ocean. They are mostly small and lie in groups; that is, several clustered together."

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas "The West: Political Map" p.129 and "California" p.135
1\. Locate Los Angeles on the first map, and then on the 2nd map. Use the California map for the rest of the questions.
2\. Locate islands off the southern California Coast. (Channel Islands)
3\. Within those, locate Santa Catalina Island.
4\. From Los Angeles, which direction do you travel to get to Catalina Island? (southwest)
5\. How could you travel to Catalina from Los Angeles? (boat or plane)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.14-15 "The next morning" - "plane over the water."

Catalina Island, Continued
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, 50 Adventures in the 50 States, My U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
Have you ever been to an island? If you lived on an island, what are some things you could do for fun? Let’s see what Tom and Ruth do at Catalina Island.

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “California” p.135
1\. Locate (Santa) Catalina Island.
2\. Do you see other islands near Catalina? Name some. (Santa Rosa, Santa Cruz, and San Clemente.)
3\. There is a name for this archipelago (group of islands). Can you find it? (Channel Islands)
4\. What ocean are they located in? (Pacific Ocean)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country p.15-16 "After the plane" - "I'm playing island!"

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My U.S. Map
Add and label Catalina Island.

➜ READ
50 Adventures in the 50 States
p.14-15 Venice Beach is right next to Los Angeles. Look at the page spread and read about surfing there.

Redwoods
⍞ Materials: Redwoods, United States Atlas

➜ INTRO
We are going to spend some time exploring a different part of the California coast.

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “California” p.135
1\. Locate the key. Find the symbols for National Park Service Units, National Forests, National Wildlife Refuges, and National Marine Sanctuaries.
2\. Some of the Channel Islands are part of a National Park. Locate and name the park. (Santa Rosa and Santa Cruz islands are part of Channel Islands National Park.)
3\. Traveling along the coast, see how many parks, forests, and refuges you can find.
4\. Locate Redwood National Park in the northern end of California.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Redwoods
p.1-11 (The book is not numbered, so p.1 is considered the page with only the boy sitting on the bench noticing the book.)
"The coast redwoods" - "two feet per year."

Redwoods
⍞ Materials: Redwoods, United States Atlas

➜ MAP QUESTION
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “The Physical United States: Climate” p.10-11
1\. Locate California. (Check p.14 if you are not sure where it is.)
2\. Remember that Redwood National Forest is on the coast in very Northern California. What is the climate there? Look at the key. (marine west coast)
3\. What do you think that means for the Redwood Forest? (a lot of rain)
4\. Locate your state and the general area where you live. How does your climate compare to that in Northern California?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Redwoods
p.12-22 "Redwoods also" - "endangered species."

Redwoods
⍞ Materials: Redwoods, United States Atlas

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “The Physical United States: Physical Map” p.8-9
1\. Locate California. (Check p.14 if you are not sure where it is.)
2\. Remember that Redwood National Forest is on the coast in very Northern California. What does the key tell us about the vegetation there? (needleleaf forest)
3\. What type of tree is a Redwood? Hint: What type of leaves does it have? (needleleaf)
4\. Locate your state and the general area where you live. How does your natural vegetation compare to that in Northern California?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Redwoods
p.23-end "When a redwood" - "fit right in."

Maps
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “How to Use This Atlas” p.7
1\. What do the symbols for cities look like? (Dots/circles)
2\. Why are the dots different sizes? (Population of the city)
3\. What symbol represents a state capital? (Circled star) National capital? (Star inside double circle.)
⍞ Map Resource: “Your State” (United States Atlas state pages)
1\. Locate your hometown.
2\. Is your town or city (or closest municipality) labeled? Why or why not?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.17-18 "It was the day" - "stands for Los Angeles."

• GEOGRAPHY WORK
Get a map of your city or local jurisdiction and explore it, looking for familiar places. Paper maps may be available from the local Planning Department or Chamber of Commerce.

Map Symbols
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas

➜ INTRO
Last time, Tom found his city—Los Angeles. This week, he will learn a little more about the map.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.18-20 "'Now', said Father" - "as it can be!"

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “The Political United States" p.14-15
1\. Locate Washington, D.C.
2\. Locate Los Angeles.
3\. Look across the United States and locate the state of Maryland.
4\. Locate Washington, D.C. What symbol represents it? (Double circle with large star inside.)
5\. Locate Washington, D.C., on the inside front cover map of Cross-Country.
6\. Do you know what that symbol means? (Country’s capital)

Map Work
⍞ Materials: Globe or World Wall Map

➜ DISCUSS
1\. In the last reading, did you notice why Ruth responded to Tom’s question the way she did?
2\. Notice that Mr. Page explained to Tom that blue “stands for” oceans, lakes, and rivers.
3\. How does Tom try to explain the way cities and water are represented on the map?
4\. Why do maps use symbols? Why is it important to know what those symbols represent?
5\. We are going to spend time looking at the land and the "blue" areas today.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Equator.
2\. Where is the Northern Hemisphere? Southern Hemisphere?
3\. Where is the North Pole? South Pole?
4\. We are going to locate the 7 continents. Locate North America, South America, Europe, Asia, Australia, Africa, and Antarctica.
5\. We are going to locate the 4 (or 5) named oceans of the world. Locate the Atlantic Ocean.
6\. Locate the Pacific Ocean.
7\. Locate the Indian Ocean.
8\. Locate the Arctic Ocean.
9\. Locate the Southern Ocean. (National Geographic atlases do not recognize the Southern Ocean as its own distinct ocean.)
10\. Some people divide the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans into northern and southern parts. Where is the North Atlantic Ocean? South Atlantic Ocean? North Pacific Ocean? South Pacific Ocean?
11\. Which ocean is the northernmost? (Arctic) The southernmost? (Southern)
12\. Which continent is the largest? (Asia) The smallest? (Australia)
13\. Which ocean is the largest? (Pacific) The smallest? (Arctic)
14\. Locate the Atlantic Ocean. It is bounded on the east by which continent(s)? (Europe and Africa.)
15\. The Atlantic Ocean is bounded on the west by which continent(s)? (North and South America.)
16\. Locate the Pacific Ocean. Which continent(s) borders the Pacific Ocean on the east? (North and South America.) On the west? (Asia and Australia.)
17\. Locate the Indian Ocean. Which continent(s) border the Indian Ocean on the east? (Asia and Australia.) On the west? (Africa) On the north? (Asia)
18\. Locate the Arctic Ocean, and identify which continent(s) it borders. (North America, Europe, and Asia.)
19\. Locate the area of water known as the Southern Ocean, and identify which continent(s) it borders. (Antarctica. Some maps from Australia consider the Southern Ocean extending as far north as its southern coast.)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Try to pay attention to where water stays after the rain; where it pools. Don't forget to keep notes in your geography or nature journal.

Globe
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, globe

➜ INTRO
Today, we will go over some information that you may already know, but enjoy it as a review if you do.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe
1\. Imagine a ball for the first few questions.
2\. Does a ball have a top and a bottom? (Yes, but you can easily turn it to change what is "top" and "bottom")
3\. Does it have an end? (No.)
4\. Are you able to see all of the ball at one time? (No, because you can only see the part facing you.)
5\. Is there any way to see all of it at once? (No. If you turn it in any direction, there is still a part you can’t see.)
6\. Now look at the globe. How does the globe compare to a ball? How are they the same? Different?
7\. Are you able to see all parts of the globe at once?
8\. What do you already know about a globe?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.21-24 "Can you think" - "center of the earth."

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Cross-Country

· Redwoods: Tell what you remember about the biome of a Redwood Forest. What do you remember about redwoods and the things that live in or near them?

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Map of Your Street

PREP: Map: Google Maps (Street View), Google Earth, Bing Maps (Streetside), a map application, or some other image that will allow an eye-level view of your home. This will be used during this lesson and the next.

➜ INTRO
Could you see your street on the maps in the last lesson? (No, the maps have a large scale.) Today we will be able to see your street and even your home.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: “Your street and house” (Use a phone or computer to view a street-level image.)
1\. Find your home. (This is a good time to learn your home address.)
2\. Can you see your neighbor’s home? Your street?
3\. Zoom out. What happens to the image? What changes? Can you see your home?
4\. Zoom out enough for it to look similar to your state map in the United States Atlas.
5\. Zoom bit by bit all the way back in. What happens?
6\. Why do you think your house/street is not on the atlas map?

➜ NOTE
Next week, you will need a printed copy of the street view of this in order to do the pet map of the neighborhood.
You can also use time in the afternoons this week, interviewing neighbors about their pets (what types and how many) to be added to a map next week.

Pet Map
⍞ Materials: Google Map (Street View)

PREP: Print the Google Maps (Street View), Google Earth, Bing Maps (Streetside), a map application, or some other image that will allow a view of your immediate neighborhood. If you live in an apartment building, it might be easier to print a photo of the front of the building and note which apartments are which.

➜ INTRO
It would be fun to see what pets live in your neighborhood, and to add them to your Google Map you explored last week. If you have not already, interview neighbors about what types of pets they have and how many.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Google Map of the neighborhood
1\. Create a key showing symbols for each type of pet.
2\. Add the symbols to the neighbors' houses/apartments on your map.
3\. If there is more than 1 pet per residence, add multiple symbols to each residence.
4\. If you would like, share your map with a family member or friend and tell them what you learned about your neighbors.

Latitude and Longitude
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ INTRO
We are going to spend some time today learning more about the lines on your globe.

➜ MAP WORK
• Latitude
1\. Locate the Equator, Tropic of Capricorn, and Tropic of Cancer.
2\. Locate 2 more named lines of latitude. (Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle)
3\. Is the Arctic Circle located in the Northern or Southern Hemisphere? (Northern)
4\. Which other major circle of latitude is located in the Northern Hemisphere? (Tropic of Cancer)
5\. Which 2 major circles of latitude are located in the Southern Hemisphere? (Antarctic Circle and Tropic of Capricorn.)
6\. Name the continents that touch the Arctic Circle. (North America, Europe, and Asia.) The Antarctic Circle? (Antarctica)
• Longitude
1\. Locate the prime meridian. (look in England)
2\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both North and South America. (Roughly between 35°W and 81°W.)
3\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both Asia and Australia. (Roughly between 114°E and 153°E.)
4\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both Europe and Africa. (Roughly between 17°W and 52°E.)
5\. Can you locate a line of longitude that passes through Antarctica but no other continents? (No)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Next week, we will have our lessons on how water moves. Make sure you have your notes ready from previous rain walks.

Map Projections
⍞ Materials: Globe, World Map

➜ INTRO
We are going to compare your globe to your wall map of the world.

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Locate the 7 continents on your globe, and then on the wall map.
2\. Compare the sizes of each continent on the globe. Now compare the sizes of each continent on the wall map. Are there any differences in the relative sizes depicted on the globe versus the sizes depicted on the wall map? (For example, Africa is the 2nd largest continent on the globe. However, North America appears to be the 2nd largest continent on the wall map.)
3\. What do you notice about the differences of Antarctica? (Antarctica appears stretched on the wall map.)
4\. Are there any differences in the sizes of the oceans? (The Arctic and Southern Oceans appear larger on the wall map than their depiction on the globe.)
5\. Practical question: If you wanted to peel an orange and lay the peel flat, how could you do that? What would it look like?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
1\. Watch the following Map Projections video on YouTube.
∞ Video Link: Map Projections video
2\. Using your globe and wall map, spend the rest of your time comparing continents and countries on each. What are the biggest differences?
3\. Which projection is your wall map? (Reference the fine print on the map.)

Water Moves

PREP: This is the lesson where you will use information you have gathered while noticing what happens around your home during rain.

➜ INTRO
We have found some of the large bodies of water on our globe, oceans. We will locate other bodies of water later, but let's just focus on water today, and how it moves. Have you been paying attention to where water goes when it rains near your home?

➜ DISCUSS
1\. What do you notice when it rains? Is there a difference when it rains lightly versus heavily? Is there a difference when it is a quick shower versus rain that lasts many hours or even days?
2\. When the rain falls on the roof of your home, where does it go? (Gutters, straight off the sides, etc.)
3\. Is there any place near your home where you know you will find a puddle after the rain?
4\. Why do you think water stays in that spot? Is it a big area? (You should notice high and low places and that water runs to low places.)
5\. Is there anything else you have noticed about how water moves near your home?
6\. Do you water plants at your home? What happens when you water them? Is there a difference in watering a plant in a pot versus in the ground? How often do you have to water plants/grass outside where you live?

➜ DRAW
1\. On a piece of blank paper, the teacher can draw a general outline of the school/home from above.
2\. Have the student draw some of the places that water moves around the home/school, noting places where the water puddles. These are the low places.

Geography Model
⍞ Materials: something large and flat; either a cookie sheet or cutting board, kinetic sand or clay, sink, water source

➜ INTRO
Today, we will watch water move on a small scale.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Tilt the flat surface into the sink so that the outside end is 1 inch higher than the end in the sink.
2\. Pour water slowly onto the raised end and note what it does.
3\. Raise the outside end to about 3 inches, and then 6 inches, pouring water slowly both times at the same spot. Do you notice any differences? (water moves faster the higher it starts)
4\. Lay the surface flat, dry it, and build a few mountains in the center of it, making a place for a "river" to run.
5\. Re-do steps 1 through 3 and make notes on what you see. Does the water adjust its course as it reaches the mountains? Does it follow the course you thought it would?

Rain Gauge
⍞ Materials: empty jar with a wide opening, plastic ruler, masking tape, and pen or Sharpie

➜ INTRO
Today, we will make a simple rain gauge.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Set up your jar or can outside to collect rainwater.
2\. Place a ruler inside the jar and tape it on the outside.
3\. Use the ruler to mark 1/4 inch, 1/2 inch, 3/4 inch, and 1 inch. Continue by quarter inches up to 2 inches.
4\. You will check the rain jar daily to see how much it has rained. Make notes on a page in your geography journal about how much it rains (when it rains). Don't forget to empty the jar after you make note of the amount, so that you start again from zero.
5\. At the end of each week and month, add up how much it has rained in total.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the US Precipitation Map to see how much it rains near Los Angeles, California. Also, look at your area on the map. Are they similar or different?
∞ Image Link: US Precipitation Map

An Orange Grove and Water

3\. Locate Los Angeles in the United States Atlas.
4\. Do you think they will still be in California?
5\. Parents must do this: use the scale in the United States Atlas to show about how far 25 miles is from Los Angeles. This will show clearly that they are still very close to Los Angeles.
6\. Do you remember the type of fruit trees that grew in the Pages' yard in Los Angeles? (Lemons and oranges.)
7\. Do you think the same fruit will grow here that grew in their yard?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.26-27 "An hour after" - "orange juice."

➜ DISCUSS
Geography is the study of place. Based on what we just learned from the visit with Mr. Green, how have California orange farmers learned to alter their environment/place in order to grow their crops?

⍞ Map Resource: My U.S. Map
Add a drawing of an orange or orange tree immediately east of Los Angeles.

Water Cycle

Do a drawing of the water cycle together, making sure the main points of evaporation and condensation, along with precipitation, runoff/collection, etc., are covered. Water is always moving.

Water Cycle Model

The Desert

Linear Map
⍞ Materials: Geography journal

➜ INTRO
Last year, you made a map of your room or schoolroom. Today, we will start a map of a hall or a linear area of your home or school.

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. Choose an area to map that is in a line (referred to as linear). For example, a hall.
2\. Begin to draw your linear space in your Geography journal.
3\. Make sure to represent anything in the hall that takes up space on the floor. (any tables, bookshelves, carpet, etc.)
4\. Next lesson, we will add any doors that connect to the hall (or other linear space). This will take 2 lessons, so we will finish it next week.

Linear Map
⍞ Materials: Geography journal

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. We are going to continue your “Linear map” in your geography journal.
2\. Now, we will add any doors that connect to the hall (or other linear space).
3\. Make sure to look back at the real space as you draw in order to keep things in order.
4\. When you are done, feel free to color your map, if desired.

➜ NOTE
If the student really enjoys this, feel free to do other spaces that they are familiar with, such as a hall at a friend’s or family member’s house. It might be a fun challenge to see what they remember about a familiar house, and then to go see it and map it. Did they remember the correct order of rooms off the hall?

Linear Street Map
⍞ Materials: wooden blocks, Duplos or Legos attached to each other, or small boxes (like the size of single-serving cereal boxes), paper, pencil, or colored pencils

➜ NOTE
This map project will take 4 lessons. The focus again is to keep things in a linear order.

➜ INTRO
We are going to make another type of “linear” map. This time, we will be using blocks with pictures taped on them to make a 3-dimensional map.

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. Choose an area that you can see from your home/school, or very close to it. (It can be the street where you live, or possibly a small strip of stores.)
2\. Last year, you may have made a map of your house and a couple of neighbors. This year, you will draw more. Decide if you want to make a map of just one side of the street or both sides.
3\. Spend several minutes really looking at the area you chose. Ask questions like...How many buildings are there? Are the buildings all the same size? If not, which ones are bigger? And how much bigger?
4\. Pay attention to the order the buildings are in.
5\. Draw a simple picture of each home/building and tape it to a block.

➜ NOTE
Next week, the student will finish drawing the homes/buildings.

Linear Street Map, Continued
⍞ Materials: wooden blocks, Duplos or Legos attached to each other, or small boxes (like the size of single-serving cereal boxes), paper, pencil, or colored pencils

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Finish drawing the pictures of homes/buildings and taping to the blocks.
2\. Teacher and student work together to set up the blocks/boxes to match the street.

➜ NOTE
Next lesson, you will begin drawing a map of your “street.”

Linear Street Map Continued
⍞ Materials: blocks with pictures from the previous lesson, geography journal, pencil, or colored pencils

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Set up your street view with the blocks again.
2\. Draw in your geography journal a simple map of your street (blocks). (Note: You can choose to draw only one side of the street or both sides.)
3\. Pay attention as you draw your map. Does it match the blocks? Are they in the right order?

➜ NOTE
The drawing of the Street View Map will continue next lesson. Also, we will move the blocks and set them up again, just using the map from your geography journal.

Linear Street Map, Conclusion
⍞ Materials: blocks with pictures from the previous lesson, a pencil or colored pencils, Geography journal

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Finish drawing into your geography journal a simple map of your street (blocks). (Note: You can choose to draw only one side of the street or both sides.)
2\. Pay attention as you draw your map. Does it match the blocks? Are they in the right order?
3\. Now, move the blocks and mix them up.
4\. Only using the Street View Map that you made in your geography journal, set up the blocks in the correct order again.
5\. If time allows, go outside and see if your drawn Street View Map matches the “real thing.”

Hoover Dam
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
I wonder how long the Pages were in the desert!

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas "The West: Physical Map" p.128 and “Nevada” p.145
1\. Locate the Mojave Desert.
2\. The Mojave Desert is primarily located in which two (2) states? (California and Nevada)
3\. Name the lake east of the Mojave Desert. (Lake Mead)
4\. Can you name the river that Lake Mead is on? (Colorado River)
5\. The Colorado River is part of the border of Nevada. It separates Nevada from what other state? (Arizona)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.33 “A drink was” - “‘going to see tomorrow.’”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My U.S. Map
Label Arizona.

➜ OBSERVE
If time permits, recreate what Ruth and her father saw in the tub. You can use a tub or sink.

⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “Nevada” p.145
1\. Locate the Colorado River in southernmost Nevada.
2\. Locate Lake Mead.
3\. There is a large dam at Lake Mead. Locate and name the dam. (Hoover Dam)
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “The Physical United States” p.8-9
1\. Locate the Colorado River in the western United States. Find where it begins in Colorado.
2\. Run your finger along the entire Colorado River. You are not able to see the entire thing because it flows into Mexico.
3\. Teacher: Help the student locate Lake Mead on the map by comparing it to the map of Nevada.
⍞ Map Resource: Inside front of Cross-Country
1\. Can you find the beginning (headwaters/source) of the Colorado River on this map?
2\. Follow the river to Lake Mead. Do you see the Hoover Dam?
3\. If you continue to follow the Colorado River, where does it go? (Through Mexico, emptying into the Gulf of California.)
4\. The Colorado River has gone from the Rocky Mountains in Colorado all the way to the ocean!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Cross-Country
p.34 “The next day” - “‘end of the bathtub!’”

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My U.S. Map

2\. Add and label the Colorado River.

Lake Mead and Great Salt Lake
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map, 50 Adventures in the 50 States

➜ INTRO
Have you ever been to a lake? What did you do there?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Inside front of Cross-Country
1\. Locate Lake Mead
2\. Lake Mead forms a border between which two states? (Nevada and Arizona)
3\. Follow the red line from Lake Mead. What is the next lake the Pages visit? (Great Salt Lake)
4\. Which state is the Great Salt Lake in? (Utah)
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas “The West: Physical Map” p.128
1\. Locate Great Salt Lake.
2\. Do you notice any differences between Lake Mead and the Great Salt Lake? (Great Salt Lake does not flow to the ocean.)

Cross-Country
p.35-36 “It was hot” - “Really swim!"

2\. Add and label the Great Salt Lake.

50 Adventures in the 50 States
p.92-93 Look at the page spread and read about slot canyons.

• GEOGRAPHY WORK
If there is a lake near your home, go visit. Notice if it is man-made or natural. Is it there because of a dam? Can you see the dam? Does it look like a mound of dirt and grass, or is it concrete like the Hoover Dam.

Bodies of Water Pages Visited
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country

➜ DISCUSS
From reading in the last lesson, we will compare the Page family’s visit to Lake Mead, the Great Salt Lake, and their visit to Catalina Island at the beginning of the book.
1\. Which activities were similar in all 3? (e.g., swimming), and which ones were different? (No fishing in the Great Salt Lake)
2\. What does the water in Great Salt Lake have in common with the water surrounding Catalina? (Saltwater)
3\. How is the water different? (No fish versus fish)
4\. Why might the Great Salt Lake be missing fish? (Salinity too high)
5\. What is different between Lake Mead and the Great Salt Lake? (Lake Mead: blue, freshwater, fish. Great Salt Lake: green, saltwater, no fish).
6\. Have you been to a freshwater lake or a saltwater lake?

➜ DRAW
If you have been to a lake, draw a picture of your time there in your geography journal, making sure to note on the picture if it was freshwater or saltwater. If you have not been to a lake, choose either Lake Mead or Great Salt Lake, and draw what you would do there.

Continents
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, Globe

When doing our map work, sometimes we have used the word "continent." Do you remember what a continent is?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Find California (and Los Angeles, if it is labeled).
2\. “Zoom out” and locate the United States.
3\. “Zoom out.” What do you call the area including the United States, Canada, and Mexico? (North America)

p.37-40 “Los Angeles” - “no one lives.”

Continents
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
(Questions taken from chapters on “Surface of the Earth” in Elementary Geography by Charlotte Mason)
1\. Which continents are upon the Equator? (Africa and South America)
2\. Which three continents are north of the Equator? (North America, Europe, Asia)
3\. Which two lie south of the Equator? (Australia and Antarctica)
4\. Which is the longest [pair of] continent(s) from north to south? (North and South America)
5\. What continent lies east of Europe? (Asia)
6\. What continent lies west of Europe? (North America)
7\. What continent is to the south of Europe? (Africa)
8\. Which continent(s) are quite surrounded by water? (Australia and Antarctica)
• Let’s further explore what Ms. Mason was asking about continents.
1\. Look on the globe, and locate where North America and South America are connected. (Panama/Colombia)
2\. Where are Africa and Asia connected? (Egypt/Israel)
3\. Which two continents do not have an obvious division and look like one large continent? (Europe and Asia)

Rocky Mountains National Park

Canyons

Rocky Mountains
⍞ Materials: Cross-Country, United States Atlas, My U.S. Map

➜ INTRO
Today we will spend our time using maps.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Inside front of Cross-Country
1\. Locate the Great Salt Lake.
2\. Name the city found along the red route near the lake.
3\. The next stop on the Page’s route is Rocky Mountain National Park. Follow the red line from the Great Salt Lake to Rocky Mountain National Park.
4\. What do you notice, first to the north, and then to the south, of their route? (Mountains)
5\. These mountains are a part of what large mountain range? (Rocky Mountains)
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas "Colorado" p.137 (5th edition)
∞ PDF link: 5th edition map
1\. Locate Rocky Mountain National Park. What color is this area on the map? (Yellow)
2\. Using the map key on p.7 (United States Atlas), what does that yellow color represent? (Look through the list and see if National Park is an option. Everything represented by yellow falls under the National Park System.)
3\. There is a large area of green on this map of Colorado, some of it surrounding the Rocky Mountain National Park. Using the map key again on p.7, what does this green represent? (National Forest)
4\. Now, using the Economy Symbols map key on p.137, what are the most prevalent symbols inside the green area? (Mining and tourism) Discuss why those economies are found there.
5\. Now, what animal-related economy symbols do you find in or just outside of the green area? (Ensure that you notice the sheep, which will show up in Cross-Country.)
6\. Discuss why sheep are found there but not other livestock, like cows. (It is okay if you cannot think of a reason or if they do not know the difference in how sheep and cattle pasture. Nonetheless, the question does help them notice geographic differences in livestock distribution. General thoughts can be that cattle require larger areas for grazing than sheep; cattle eat grass, whereas sheep can consume a wide range of weeds; sheep can navigate hilly, steep terrain that cattle cannot.)

Pacing and Scale Map
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps, a large piece of paper (can be a large piece of art paper, legal-size paper, etc.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-9 "Say you want" - "Label it."

➜ PRACTICE
Spend a couple of minutes practicing pacing. This means that you practice keeping the same length/pace of steps. Take 10 steps away from your home, and try to take 10 to get back to the same spot.

➜ MAP DRAWING
Today, we will begin a new map of your home and your neighbors. Your map will only be as big as your piece of paper.
1\. The book said that 25 steps are the same as an inch.
2\. Pace the outside general area of your home, and draw it on one of the middle edges of your paper. (For example, if it takes 50 paces to walk across the front of your home, that line would be 2 inches long on the paper. If the width is 25 steps, that will be one inch. Please don't worry about being super precise; we just want to begin building the idea of scale.)
3\. After you have drawn your home, begin to move outwards. (You will have another lesson to finish this, and you are free to work on it in the afternoons or during assessment week.)
4\. First, you will need to do things like pace the space between your home and the nearest neighbor. Then, pace the outside of your neighbor's house. (Make sure to get permission.)
5\. Work on it until your class time is up.

Scale Map, Continued

➜ MAP DRAWING
Today, we continue your scale map of your home and your neighbors.
1\. Don't forget that 25 steps equal an inch on your map.
2\. Assess how far you went last lesson.
3\. Continue to move outward from your home. Work on it for the class time, trying to add at least 2 neighbors.
4\. Do you notice that scale mapping is much more time-consuming? I wonder how long geographers have to work on a map, and if there are things they learn to do it more quickly.
5\. If you want to continue your map, feel free to use the afternoon time.
